title: Bonfire toffee
subtitle: Dark treacle toffee for Guy Fawkes Night
excerpt: Black treacle, golden syrup, and demerara sugar cooked slowly to hard-crack stage, then poured into a tin and broken into dark, bitter-edged shards. A traditional British confection eaten on 5 November.
sourceNotes: Bonfire toffee (also called plot toffee or treacle toffee) is a traditional British confection associated with Guy Fawkes Night, 5 November. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), and Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, both document treacle toffee under the confectionery chapter. The tradition of eating treacle toffee on 5 November is documented from the mid-nineteenth century.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. Use a larger heavy-based pan to prevent boilover.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps wrapped in waxed paper in a dry tin for up to 2 weeks. Do not refrigerate.

glossaryTerms.definition: The confectionery temperature stage reached when sugar syrup hits 149-154°C. A drop in cold water forms brittle threads that snap cleanly. Toffee cooked to hard-crack sets firm and shatters when bitten rather than bending or sticking to teeth.

content.text: Bonfire toffee is made almost entirely from black treacle and demerara sugar, cooked slowly to
and poured into a tin to set. The treacle gives it a deeply bitter, almost burnt edge that cuts through the sweetness. It is not a gentle confection. Eat it in small pieces.
Boiling sugar reaches 150°C and sticks to skin. Keep children away from the hob during cooking. If hot toffee contacts skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes.
Line a 20 cm square cake tin with baking paper and set it near the hob. Have a sugar thermometer clipped to the pan or a digital thermometer ready.

Put the {{demerara-sugar}}, {{black-treacle}}, {{golden-syrup}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, {{white-wine-vinegar}}, and 3 tbsp of water in a medium heavy-based saucepan. Stir over a low heat until the sugar dissolves and the butter melts. Bring to a boil over medium heat, then cook without stirring until the thermometer reads 149-154°C (hard-crack stage). The mixture will be very dark. This takes 20-25 minutes; resist the urge to rush it with high heat, which causes uneven cooking and burning.

Pour the toffee into the prepared tin. Leave to cool at room temperature for 45 minutes until fully set and hard. Break into irregular pieces by pressing a blunt knife across the surface and snapping. Wrap individual pieces in squares of waxed paper if storing.
